_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against AGA Clinical Practice Update on Diverticular Disease, NICE NG147 Diverticular Disease, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Diverticulitis: Symptoms, Types, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Diverticulitis is inflammation and/or infection of diverticula — small pouches that form herniations through the muscular wall of the colon, most commonly in the sigmoid (left lower quadrant) colon. While diverticulosis (the presence of diverticula without inflammation) is present in over 60% of people over age 60 and is largely asymptomatic, diverticulitis develops when a diverticulum becomes blocked by trapped stool, leading to bacterial overgrowth, inflammation, micro-perforation, and potentially serious complications including abscess, perforation, peritonitis, fistula formation, and bowel obstruction. The classic presentation is left lower quadrant pain, fever, and leukocytosis — but modern evidence has challenged many long-held assumptions about the disease, including the belief that nuts, seeds, and popcorn cause attacks. Management has shifted toward conservative treatment for uncomplicated cases, with antibiotics no longer routinely recommended for all presentations.

keyStats.prevalence: Diverticulosis affects 60-70% of adults by age 60 and 80% by age 80; only 10-25% of people with diverticulosis ever develop symptomatic diverticular disease, and 1-5% develop acute diverticulitis
keyStats.demographics: Diverticulitis is slightly more common in males before age 60; after age 60, women have equal or slightly higher rates. Western populations have the highest rates; diverticulosis is rare in Africa and Asia.
keyStats.avgAge: Acute diverticulitis typically presents between ages 50-70; the incidence rises sharply after age 40 and peaks at 60-70 years
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 200,000-300,000 hospitalizations for acute diverticulitis annually in the United States; 25-30% of people hospitalized for diverticulitis require surgery

definition: Diverticulitis (ICD-10: K57 for all diverticular disease; K57.2 for diverticulitis of large intestine with perforation and abscess; K57.8 for other diverticular disease) is inflammation of colonic diverticula — outpouchings of mucosa and submucosa that herniate through the muscularis propria at points of weakness where the vasa recta penetrate the muscle wall. Diverticula form preferentially in the sigmoid colon where intraluminal pressure is highest, though they can occur anywhere in the colon. The left colon (sigmoid and descending) is the most common site in Western populations; the right colon is more commonly affected in Asian populations. Diverticulitis occurs when a diverticulum becomes obstructed by fecalith or thick stool, leading to bacterial proliferation, mucosal inflammation, and if untreated, perforation. The disease spectrum ranges from asymptomatic (diverticulosis) to simple uninflamed diverticula, to symptomatic uncomplicated diverticular disease, to acute diverticulitis (with or without complications).

typesClassification.description: The presence of colonic diverticula without symptoms. Discovered incidentally on colonoscopy or CT. Requires no treatment — only dietary fiber optimization for prevention. Patients should be unaware they have it; most never develop problems.

Chronic or recurrent abdominal pain attributable to diverticula without evidence of acute inflammation (no fever, no leukocytosis, no localized peritoneal signs). May present with altered bowel habit, bloating, and intermittent LLQ pain. Requires medical management and dietary modification.

Acute inflammation of a diverticulum without complications (no abscess, perforation, fistula, or obstruction). Presents with LLQ pain, fever, and leukocytosis. CT confirms the diagnosis and excludes complications. Managed with or without antibiotics.

Diverticulitis with local complications: Hinchey I is a pericolic abscess (confined to the mesentery); Hinchey II is a pelvic abscess or retroperitoneal abscess. Both can often be managed with percutaneous drainage and antibiotics; surgery is reserved for failure of percutaneous management.

Hinchey III is purulent peritonitis (feces not spilling but pus present); Hinchey IV is fecal peritonitis (stool spilling into the peritoneal cavity). Both are surgical emergencies requiring urgent resection. Mortality is 5-15% for Hinchey III, 25-40% for Hinchey IV.

Diverticula can bleed painlessly — typically a brisk, painless, bright red or maroon lower GI bleed from the vasa recta that runs along the diverticulum base. Bleeding is arterial, often brisk, and accounts for 20-30% of lower GI bleeds. Unlike diverticulitis, diverticular bleeding is not preceded by inflammation.

primarySymptoms: Left lower quadrant abdominal pain — the most common presenting symptom of diverticulitis, present in 70-90% of cases. Pain is typically constant (not cramping), localized to the LLQ where the sigmoid colon is located, and may have been present for several days before presentation.
Fever — present in 80-90% of acute diverticulitis cases. Fever reflects the systemic inflammatory response to infection; temperatures >38.5°C suggest more severe disease or abscess.

Leukocytosis — an elevated white blood cell count (typically 11,000-20,000 cells/microliter) reflects the acute inflammatory/infectious response. Absent leukocytosis should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses.
Nausea and vomiting — nonspecific symptoms reflecting the intra-abdominal inflammatory process. Vomiting may indicate more advanced disease with obstruction or peritonitis.

Altered bowel habit — constipation is common (slowed transit through an inflamed segment), though diarrhea can occur from the inflammatory exudate. A change in baseline bowel pattern is a useful symptom.

Dysuria and urinary symptoms — when an inflamed sigmoid colon lies adjacent to the bladder (a common anatomical relationship), urinary frequency, dysuria, and sterile pyuria can result from mechanical bladder irritation.
Bloody stools — not typical of uncomplicated diverticulitis; when present, it should raise concern for diverticular bleeding (a separate entity) or a coincidental colorectal malignancy.
Localized peritoneal signs — guarding and rebound tenderness over the LLQ indicate local inflammation of the parietal peritoneum covering the abdominal wall. These signs help assess disease severity.
earlyWarningSigns: New onset left lower quadrant pain in a patient over age 40 — especially with fever and leukocytosis, should trigger evaluation for acute diverticulitis
Pain that migrates from LLQ to become generalized — suggests perforation with developing peritonitis
Persistently high fever (>39°C) despite oral antibiotics — suggests abscess requiring percutaneous or surgical drainage
Progressive constipation with nausea/vomiting — may indicate partial bowel obstruction from stricture or inflammatory mass
Urinary symptoms (frequency, dysuria) accompanying LLQ pain — may indicate colovesical fistula formation (communication between colon and bladder)
emergencySigns: Generalized peritonitis with LLQ pain — signifies free perforation and peritonitis; requires immediate resuscitation and urgent surgical exploration
Hemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) with LLQ pain — suggests septic shock from perforated diverticulitis; requires immediate resuscitation and surgery
Fecal vomiting — indicates complete bowel obstruction or high-grade partial obstruction with retrograde contamination; requires urgent surgery
Massive lower GI bleeding with LLQ pain — requires urgent colonoscopy to localize bleeding source and endoscopic or surgical intervention
Severe abdominal distension with absent bowel sounds — suggests complete colonic obstruction; requires urgent surgical evaluation

causes.description: The most accepted mechanism for diverticulitis: trapped stool (fecalith) blocks the narrow neck of a diverticulum, causing mucus accumulation, bacterial overgrowth, and increased intradiverticular pressure. This leads to mucosal ischemia, inflammation, and if untreated, microperforation. Bacteria within the static stool proliferate and invade the compromised diverticular wall.

The sigmoid colon generates the highest intraluminal pressures in the colon due to its segmental contractions and smaller lumen diameter. These high pressures cause the mucosa to herniate through the weakest points of the muscular wall — the sites where the vasa recta penetrate. Persistent high pressures accelerate diverticula formation and increase the risk of fecalith trapping.

A diet chronically low in dietary fiber (roughage) produces smaller, harder stools that require higher colonic pressures to propel. This 'pressure theory' explains why diverticulosis is rare in African rural populations (high fiber diet) and common in Western populations (low fiber diet). Fiber also speeds colonic transit, reducing the time for stool to become desiccated and hard.

causes.cause: Abnormal colonic motility and smooth muscle dysfunction
causes.description: Studies of sigmoid colon smooth muscle in diverticular disease show abnormal, high-amplitude segmental contractions. This motility dysfunction may be a primary abnormality that predisposes to both diverticula formation and diverticulitis — rather than being caused by diverticula, it may be the underlying driver of the disease process.

causes.cause: Aging and structural weakening of the colonic wall
causes.description: Diverticulosis is overwhelmingly a disease of aging — the prevalence approaches 80% by age 80. With age, the colonic muscular wall weakens, collagen cross-linking increases (reducing tissue elasticity), and the vasa recta openings enlarge. These structural changes make herniation more likely. The inflammatory response to diverticular obstruction also becomes more vigorous with age.

Obesity (BMI >30) is associated with a 40-80% increased risk of diverticulitis. Visceral adiposity produces a chronic pro-inflammatory state (elevated CRP, IL-6, TNF-alpha) that may amplify the inflammatory response within a diverticulum. Metabolic syndrome independently increases diverticulitis risk.

A family history of diverticulosis or diverticulitis increases risk 2-3 fold. Studies of monozygotic twins support a heritable component to diverticular disease. Several genetic loci (particularly near the TNFSF13B gene involved in immune regulation) have been associated with diverticulosis in genome-wide association studies.

riskFactors.description: Diverticulosis prevalence increases from <10% at age 40 to 60-70% by age 60 and 80% by age 80. The risk of diverticulitis (the complication of diverticulosis) parallels this age-related increase.

Chronic inadequate fiber intake is associated with harder stools, slower transit, and higher colonic pressures. Epidemiological studies show populations with high fiber diets (rural Africa, Asia) have near-zero diverticulosis, while Western low-fiber diets correlate with 60-80% prevalence.

Obesity increases diverticulitis risk by 40-80% and increases the risk of complicated diverticulitis. Visceral fat produces a pro-inflammatory state that may amplify the inflammatory response in diverticula.

Smoking is associated with a dose-dependent increase in diverticulitis risk and an increased risk of complications including perforation. The mechanism may involve impaired tissue healing and immune dysfunction.

Regular physical activity (particularly aerobic exercise) reduces diverticulitis risk by 20-40%. Physical activity speeds colonic transit, reduces intraluminal pressure, and reduces visceral obesity — all protective mechanisms.

Regular NSAID use (aspirin, ibuprofen, naproxen) is associated with a 20-30% increased risk of diverticulitis and diverticular bleeding. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and may impair mucosal healing in diverticula.
A 2-3 fold increased risk in first-degree relatives of patients with diverticulosis or diverticulitis. The genetic contribution is estimated at 20-30% heritability.

Red meat, processed meat, high-fat foods, and refined carbohydrates are associated with increased diverticulitis risk. A Western dietary pattern drives metabolic syndrome, low-grade inflammation, and constipation.

Chronic constipation with straining produces high colonic pressures and harder stools that more easily become trapped in diverticula. Treating constipation reduces intraluminal pressure and may prevent recurrent diverticulitis.

Chronic corticosteroid use, organ transplant immunosuppression, and chemotherapy significantly increase the risk of complicated diverticulitis and free perforation. The inflammatory response is blunted, and perforations are more likely to be silent until catastrophic.

affectedDemographics: Diverticulosis affects 60-70% of adults by age 60 in Western countries — making it one of the most common colonic conditions in the developed world
Diverticulitis is relatively uncommon before age 40 (<5% of cases), but incidence is rising in younger adults due to obesity and metabolic syndrome
Western populations (North America, Europe, Australia) have the highest rates; diverticulosis is rare in rural Africa and Asia where high-fiber diets are the norm
Diverticular disease is slightly more common in men before age 60; after age 60, women have equal or higher rates
Obesity, metabolic syndrome, and low fiber diets in younger adults are shifting the epidemiology toward younger presentation
Complicated diverticulitis (Hinchey III-IV) is more common in immunocompromised patients and the elderly
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of acute diverticulitis is primarily clinical (LLQ pain + fever + leukocytosis) but confirmed by CT abdomen with contrast (the diagnostic gold standard — >95% sensitivity and specificity). CT classifies disease severity, identifies complications (abscess, perforation, fistula, obstruction), and excludes alternative diagnoses. The Hinchey classification system (I-IV) based on CT findings guides management: Stage I/II with abscess <4 cm may be managed medically; Hinchey III/IV requires surgery. Colonoscopy should be performed 6-8 weeks after resolution to exclude underlying colorectal malignancy — a critical consideration because diverticulitis and colorectal cancer can present identically. In the acute setting, colonoscopy is contraindicated due to perforation risk. Laboratory evaluation includes CBC (leukocytosis), CRP (elevated in diverticulitis, useful for severity assessment), and basic metabolic panel.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: The diagnostic gold standard for acute diverticulitis. CT confirms the diagnosis (>95% sensitivity and specificity), classifies severity (Hinchey I-IV), identifies complications (abscess, free air, fistula), and excludes alternative diagnoses (appendicitis, ovarian pathology, colorectal cancer).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute CT scan with IV contrast (and sometimes oral contrast). Requires an IV line. You will lie on a scanning table that moves through a donut-shaped scanner. Results are available within 1-2 hours. No fasting required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Leukocytosis (elevated WBC) is present in 80-90% of acute diverticulitis cases. A left shift (elevated neutrophils with band cells) suggests bacterial infection. Anemia may indicate chronic blood loss from concurrent colonic disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Results available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: CRP is elevated in acute diverticulitis and is useful for assessing severity — CRP >150 mg/L at presentation is associated with increased risk of complicated diverticulitis. CRP trending down is a marker of treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw, often combined with CBC. Results available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Indicated 6-8 weeks after an episode of acute diverticulitis to exclude underlying colorectal neoplasia (cancer or advanced adenoma). Diverticulitis and colorectal cancer can present identically; imaging cannot reliably distinguish them.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible colonoscope is passed through the entire colon after bowel preparation (clear liquids + bowel-cleansing agents). Takes 30-60 minutes. Performed under sedation. Biopsies are taken of any suspicious lesions. Colonoscopy is CONTRAINDICATED in acute diverticulitis due to perforation risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A limited study for suspected bowel obstruction. May show dilated colonic loops, air-fluid levels, or in free perforation, free intraperitoneal air (pneumoperitoneum). Not useful for diagnosing uncomplicated diverticulitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A quick plain X-ray of the abdomen (standing and supine films). No preparation needed. Results within minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Can identify thickened bowel loops and pericolic inflammation in the sigmoid region. More useful in thin patients and for evaluating reproductive-age women to exclude ovarian pathology. Operator-dependent; less sensitive than CT for deep pelvic abscesses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute non-invasive scan using a transducer on the abdomen. No radiation, no contrast.

treatmentOverview: The treatment of diverticulitis has evolved significantly based on modern evidence. For uncomplicated acute diverticulitis (Hinchey I without abscess), a trial of management without antibiotics showed non-inferiority to antibiotic treatment in several RCTs — antibiotics are no longer routinely recommended for uncomplicated diverticulitis in immunocompetent patients. Treatment consists of bowel rest (clear liquids), analgesia (acetaminophen preferred; NSAIDs avoided), and clinical follow-up. Complicated diverticulitis with abscess (Hinchey I >4 cm, Hinchey II) requires antibiotics and/or percutaneous drainage. Free perforation (Hinchey III/IV) requires urgent surgical resection — either primary anastomosis or Hartmann's procedure (colostomy creation). For recurrent diverticulitis (2+ episodes), elective surgical resection is often recommended, though the risk-benefit of surgery versus continued medical management must be individualized. Mesalamine and rifaximin have been studied for preventing recurrent diverticulitis with modest results.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive care without antibiotics (for uncomplicated acute diverticulitis)
medicalTreatments.description: A liquid diet, oral analgesia (acetaminophen), and clinical monitoring without systemic antibiotics is now standard for uncomplicated diverticulitis in immunocompetent patients based on evidence that antibiotics do not alter the natural history of uncomplicated disease. Patients must be able to tolerate oral intake and have reliable follow-up.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Multiple RCTs (the AVOD trial, DIABISO, and others) have shown non-inferiority of non-antibiotic management for uncomplicated diverticulitis, with resolution rates of 90-95% and no increased risk of complications compared to antibiotic-treated patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum oral antibiotics (for complicated or severe diverticulitis)
medicalTreatments.description: For patients with abscess >4 cm, complicated diverticulitis, or unable to tolerate oral intake: oral or IV broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negative, gram-positive, and anaerobic bacteria (e.g., ciprofloxacin + metronidazole, or amoxicillin-clavulanate) for 7-14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotics are indicated for complicated diverticulitis (abscess, perforation) and for immunocompromised patients with diverticulitis. They should not be used routinely for uncomplicated diverticulitis — this is a key change from historical practice.

medicalTreatments.name: Percutaneous abscess drainage (CT or ultrasound-guided)
medicalTreatments.description: For diverticular abscesses >4 cm, image-guided percutaneous drainage with a pigtail catheter inserted through the abdominal wall into the abscess cavity provides source control, allowing resolution without surgery in 80-90% of cases. The drain remains until outputs are minimal and imaging confirms resolution.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Technical success rates of 80-90%; clinical success (avoiding surgery) in 70-80% of selected patients. Allows the acute episode to resolve and the bowel to quieten before elective surgical resection if needed.

medicalTreatments.name: Mesalamine 1.6-2.4 g/day (for preventing recurrent diverticulitis)
medicalTreatments.description: An anti-inflammatory 5-ASA agent with modest evidence for reducing recurrent diverticular symptoms and possibly recurrent diverticulitis. Proposed mechanism: reduction of gut inflammation that drives recurrent attacks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Evidence is inconsistent. Some trials (S小平 trial, PREVENT 1) showed modest reductions in recurrent symptoms; others showed no benefit. Not recommended as standard of care by most guidelines but may be considered in patients with recurrent episodes who decline or are not candidates for surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Rifaximin 550 mg twice daily (for recurrent symptomatic diverticular disease)
medicalTreatments.description: A non-absorbable rifamycin antibiotic that acts locally in the gut lumen, reducing bacterial overgrowth and bacterial translocation in diverticula. Course: 2 weeks per month for 12 months. Used for recurrent symptomatic uncomplicated diverticular disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Some RCTs show modest reduction in recurrent symptoms and diverticulitis episodes compared to fiber alone. The evidence is modest, and rifaximin is not standard of care, but it is a reasonable option for patients with recurrent symptoms who decline surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Elective sigmoid colectomy for recurrent or complicated diverticulitis
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the sigmoid colon (the most common site of diverticulitis) for patients with recurrent episodes (typically 2+ episodes), complicated diverticulitis that has been managed with percutaneous drainage, or symptoms that significantly impair quality of life. The colon is reconnected (primary anastomosis) in most cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Elective laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy for diverticulitis has a morbidity of 10-15%, mortality <1%, and excellent long-term symptom relief in 80-90% of appropriately selected patients. The risk of recurrence after colectomy is <5% at the anastomotic site.

surgicalOptions.name: Hartmann's procedure (sigmoid resection with colostomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Urgent surgical resection of the diseased sigmoid colon with creation of a temporary colostomy and closure of the rectal stump. Indicated for perforated diverticulitis with purulent or fecal peritonitis (Hinchey III/IV) in hemodynamically unstable patients. The colostomy can often be reversed (restored) in 3-6 months if the patient's condition permits.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Hartmann's procedure is the standard urgent surgical option for perforated diverticulitis with peritonitis in unstable patients. Mortality is 5-15% for Hinchey III, 25-40% for Hinchey IV. Reversal rates (colostomy takedown) are 50-70% — many patients, particularly elderly or frail patients, live with the colostomy permanently.

surgicalOptions.name: Primary anastomosis with ileostomy (for urgent diverticulitis)
surgicalOptions.description: An alternative to Hartmann's procedure for urgent/emergent diverticulitis: resection of the diseased colon with immediate reconnection (primary anastomosis) of the remaining colon to the rectum, protected by a temporary loop ileostomy. The ileostomy is reversed in 8-12 weeks after the anastomosis has healed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Studies comparing primary anastomosis + ileostomy to Hartmann's show lower permanent stoma rates (15-25% vs 50-60%) with similar mortality. Increasingly preferred in selected hemodynamically stable patients undergoing urgent surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic peritoneal lavage and drainage (investigational for selected Hinchey III)
surgicalOptions.description: Laparoscopic washout of the peritoneal cavity with irrigation and drainage, without colonic resection, for Hinchey III purulent peritonitis in selected patients. Preserves the colon and avoids colostomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Early studies showed promising results, but a 2019 Dutch RCT (DILAp) showed higher mortality with laparoscopic lavage than with primary resection. Currently not recommended as standard of care; may be considered in highly selected cases by experienced surgeons.

alternativeTreatments.name: High-fiber diet supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: A high-fiber diet (25-35 g/day of insoluble fiber from fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes) softens stools, speeds colonic transit, and reduces intraluminal pressure — the mechanisms by which high fiber diets prevent diverticulosis and reduce recurrence risk. Fiber supplementation is the cornerstone of diverticular disease prevention and management.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Epidemiological evidence strongly supports high fiber intake in reducing diverticulosis prevalence and diverticulitis risk. For preventing recurrence after an initial episode, the evidence is less robust but fiber is universally recommended by guidelines.

alternativeTreatments.name: Regular physical exercise
alternativeTreatments.description: Aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week of moderate activity) reduces diverticulitis risk by 20-40% and reduces recurrence risk. Mechanism: improved colonic motility and reduced intraluminal pressure, reduced visceral adiposity, and improved immune function.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Prospective cohort studies consistently show an inverse relationship between physical activity and diverticulitis risk. A practical, evidence-based recommendation for all patients with diverticular disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotics for symptomatic uncomplicated diverticular disease
alternativeTreatments.description: Probiotic formulations (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Streptococcus species) aim to modulate the gut microbiome and reduce gut inflammation associated with diverticular symptoms. Often combined with fiber supplementation.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Evidence is limited and inconsistent. Some small RCTs show modest symptom improvement; others show no benefit. Probiotics are safe and may help with bloating and irregular bowel habit, but are not a standard treatment for diverticulitis prevention.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist should be involved in the initial diagnosis (CT confirmation and exclusion of alternative diagnoses) and in coordinating elective colonoscopy after resolution. A colorectal surgeon should be involved for complicated diverticulitis (abscess requiring drainage, recurrent episodes, suspected perforation) and for elective colectomy evaluation. Urgent surgical consultation is required for peritonitis, sepsis, or suspected free perforation.
doctorSelectionGuide: For uncomplicated diverticulitis, your primary care physician can manage the initial episode with CT confirmation and supportive care. See a gastroenterologist if colonoscopy is needed after recovery, or for recurrent symptomatic diverticular disease. See a colorectal surgeon if you have had 2+ episodes of diverticulitis (elective colectomy discussion), a diverticular abscess requiring drainage, or any signs of complicated disease (fever not responding to antibiotics, worsening pain, peritonitis).
preventionStrategies: Maintain a high-fiber diet (25-35 g/day) — the most evidence-based strategy for preventing diverticulosis and reducing diverticulitis recurrence
Exercise regularly (150 minutes/week) — reduces intraluminal colonic pressure, improves motility, and reduces visceral obesity
Avoid smoking — smoking is a modifiable risk factor for diverticulitis and its complications
Maintain healthy weight — obesity increases diverticulitis risk by 40-80%
Avoid routine NSAID use — NSAIDs increase the risk of diverticulitis and diverticular bleeding; use acetaminophen for pain instead
Treat constipation proactively — avoid straining, use fiber supplementation or osmotic laxatives if needed
lifestyleModifications: Increase dietary fiber gradually — sudden large increases can cause bloating and flatulence; titrate up over 2-4 weeks
Stay well-hydrated — adequate water intake is essential for fiber to work effectively; fiber without water can worsen constipation
Exercise daily — even 20-30 minutes of brisk walking is beneficial for colonic motility and visceral fat reduction
Avoid prolonged sitting — physical activity breaks improve colonic motility
Use acetaminophen for pain — avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin) which increase diverticular bleeding and perforation risk
Avoid enemas and aggressive bowel preparations — these can cause perforation in a weakened diverticular colon

recommended: High-fiber diet (25-35 g/day) — insoluble fiber from whole grains, fruits, vegetables, legumes softens stool, reduces colonic pressure, and prevents diverticular complications
Fruits and vegetables — apples, pears (with skin), berries, broccoli, spinach, carrots — all excellent sources of fiber with additional prebiotic benefits
Whole grains — oats, barley, quinoa, whole wheat bread, brown rice — slow digestion, softer stools, reduced colonic pressures
Legumes — lentils, chickpeas, black beans — exceptionally high in fiber (8-10 g per cup cooked) plus resistant starch that feeds healthy gut bacteria
Gradual fiber increase with adequate water — introduce fiber supplements (psyllium) gradually to minimize gas and bloating, with 8+ glasses of water daily
avoid: Low-fiber Western diets — the single most important modifiable risk factor for diverticulosis and diverticulitis

Nuts, seeds, and popcorn — this is a MYTH; large observational studies (including the Nurses' Health Study) have NOT shown these foods increase diverticulitis risk; they are a healthy source of fiber and should not be restricted
Highly processed foods — low fiber, high fat, and refined carbohydrates drive constipation, obesity, and metabolic syndrome
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin) — increase risk of diverticular bleeding and possibly perforation; avoid regular use
Excessive red meat — high red meat intake is associated with increased diverticulitis risk in epidemiological studies
Aggressive bowel cleansing preparations — colonic irrigation, enemas, and harsh laxatives can cause perforation in diverticular disease

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is one of the most consistently evidence-based interventions for reducing diverticulitis risk and recurrence. The mechanism is multifactorial: improved colonic motility and reduced intraluminal pressure, reduced visceral obesity and chronic inflammation, and improved immune function. The recommendation is 150 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise per week (brisk walking, cycling, swimming), with resistance training twice weekly. During an acute diverticulitis episode, exercise is limited to gentle ambulation until resolution. After recovery, full exercise activity can resume.
dailyManagement: Take acetaminophen for pain rather than NSAIDs — NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin) increase diverticular bleeding and should be avoided in diverticular disease
If your episode is managed without antibiotics, monitor your temperature at home — fever returning or worsening despite conservative management means you need to return for evaluation
After recovery, schedule your elective colonoscopy for 6-8 weeks out — this must be done to exclude underlying colorectal cancer or advanced adenoma
If you have had diverticulitis, review your fiber intake — aim for 25-35 g/day of dietary fiber to prevent recurrence
Report blood in stool promptly — fresh red or maroon blood may indicate diverticular bleeding, a separate indication for urgent colonoscopy
Stay physically active — regular exercise is the most evidence-based way to prevent recurrence after an initial episode
prognosis: The prognosis for uncomplicated acute diverticulitis managed without antibiotics is excellent — 90-95% resolve within 7-10 days with conservative management. The recurrence rate after a first episode is 15-30% within 5 years. Each subsequent episode carries a higher risk of the next. Surgical resection (elective sigmoid colectomy) is curative for recurrent diverticulitis. For complicated diverticulitis with abscess, percutaneous drainage is successful in 70-80% of cases without requiring surgery. For free perforation with peritonitis (Hinchey III/IV), mortality ranges from 5-15% (Hinchey III) to 25-40% (Hinchey IV) — making early recognition and intervention critical. Chronic sequelae of recurrent diverticulitis include colonic strictures (causing obstruction) and colovesical fistulas.
recoveryTimeline: Uncomplicated diverticulitis with supportive care: improvement typically within 48-72 hours of dietary modification and analgesia; full symptom resolution in 7-14 days. A follow-up appointment at 2-4 weeks is standard. Elective colonoscopy should be performed 6-8 weeks after recovery. Percutaneous drainage: drain remains in place for 1-3 weeks until output diminishes; oral antibiotics continue for 7-14 days; elective colectomy evaluation follows once the acute episode resolves (usually 6-8 weeks). Elective colectomy: hospitalization 3-5 days; return to normal activities in 4-6 weeks. Emergency colectomy for perforated diverticulitis: hospitalization 1-2 weeks; recovery 6-12 weeks.
complications: Diverticular abscess — a collection of pus adjacent to an inflamed diverticulum (Hinchey I). Presents with fever, LLQ pain, and leukocytosis. Managed with antibiotics alone (if <4 cm) or percutaneous drainage (if >4 cm). Surgery if percutaneous management fails.

Free perforation and purulent peritonitis (Hinchey III) — rupture of a diverticulum with spillage of pus into the peritoneal cavity. Presents with sudden severe LLQ pain, peritoneal signs, fever, and sepsis. Requires urgent surgical resection and washout.

Fecal peritonitis (Hinchey IV) — the most severe complication, with perforation and spillage of fecal contents into the peritoneal cavity. Presents with shock, severe peritonitis, and high mortality. Requires emergency surgery.

Colovesical fistula — an abnormal communication between the sigmoid colon and the urinary bladder, typically from chronic recurrent diverticulitis eroding into adjacent structures. Presents with recurrent urinary tract infections, pneumaturia (air in urine), and fecaluria. Requires surgical repair.

Colonic stricture and obstruction — chronic recurrent diverticulitis can cause fibrosis and stricturing of the sigmoid colon, leading to progressive large bowel obstruction. Presents with constipation, abdominal distension, and thin-caliber stools. Requires surgery.

Diverticular bleeding — a separate entity from diverticulitis, painless brisk lower GI bleeding from the vasa recta adjacent to a diverticulum. Accounts for 20-30% of lower GI bleeds. Often requires colonoscopic hemostasis or angiographic embolization.
Recurrent diverticulitis — 15-30% of patients experience a second episode within 5 years; each episode increases the risk of the next and of complications
Chronic diverticular pain — persistent LLQ pain without acute inflammation (symptomatic uncomplicated diverticular disease) in 5-10% of patients with recurrent episodes

supportResources.name: AGA Clinical Practice Update: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Diverticulitis. American Gastroenterological Association; 2023.
supportResources.description: Expert clinical guidance on the diagnosis and management of acute diverticulitis, including the evidence base for non-antibiotic management of uncomplicated diverticulitis.

supportResources.name: NICE Guideline NG147: Diverticular Disease. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2019 (updated 2021).
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline covering the diagnosis, management, and prevention of diverticular disease, including antibiotic stewardship and indications for surgery.

supportResources.name: Strate L. Diverticular Disease: an Update on Pathophysiology and Management. Nature Reviews Gastroenterology & Hepatology. 2022;19(12):771-784.
supportResources.description: A comprehensive contemporary review of diverticular disease pathophysiology, epidemiology, and management in the light of recent RCTs challenging historical assumptions.

supportResources.name: Nurses' Health Study findings on diet and diverticular disease. Gastroenterology.
supportResources.description: Large prospective cohort data demonstrating that nuts, seeds, and popcorn do NOT increase diverticulitis risk, challenging the traditional dietary restriction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS causes chronic abdominal pain with altered bowel habits (diarrhea-predominant, constipation-predominant, or mixed) without inflammation or structural abnormality. Diverticular disease causes pain from structural inflammation. The two can overlap (post-diverticulitis IBS-type symptoms are common). CRP and imaging distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Appendicitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Appendicitis causes periumbilical pain that migrates to the right lower quadrant — the opposite anatomical pattern from diverticulitis (left lower quadrant). Both cause fever, leukocytosis, and localized peritoneal signs. CT distinguishes them reliably. The two can be confused in patients with right-sided (ascending) colonic diverticulitis, which is more common in Asian populations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ovarian Cyst or Ovarian Torsion
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: In women of reproductive age, an ovarian cyst or twisted ovary (ovarian torsion) can cause acute LLQ or RLQ pain that mimics diverticulitis. Pelvic ultrasound and CT with gynecological assessment distinguishes them. Ovarian pathology does not cause leukocytosis unless complicated by rupture or infection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Colorectal Cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Colorectal cancer can present with LLQ pain, fever, and leukocytosis that mimics diverticulitis ('masked cancer'). This is why colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after diverticulitis resolution is mandatory — CT and clinical features cannot reliably distinguish an inflamed diverticulum from a cancerous segment.

relatedConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-diverticulitis IBS-type symptoms (persistent abdominal pain, altered bowel habits) are common after an episode of diverticulitis. The two conditions can coexist, and the clinical distinction between recurrent diverticulitis and IBS can be challenging. CRP and CT help distinguish active inflammation from functional symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Colorectal cancer and diverticulitis can present with identical symptoms — this is why colonoscopy after recovery is essential. Cancer can arise in a segment of diverticular disease; the two are not mutually exclusive.

relatedConditions.name: Crohn Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Crohn disease of the colon can mimic diverticulitis (fever, LLQ pain, leukocytosis) and can coexist with diverticulosis. Colonoscopy with biopsies and CT help distinguish inflammatory bowel disease from diverticular inflammation.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Constipation
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic constipation is both a risk factor for and a consequence of diverticular disease. Straining produces high colonic pressures that drive diverticula formation and fecalith trapping. Managing constipation is central to diverticular disease prevention and management.

faqs.question: Is it true that I should avoid nuts and seeds if I have diverticulitis?
faqs.answer: No — this is a myth that has been definitively debunked. Large prospective studies, including the Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study, found that nuts, seeds, popcorn, and corn do NOT increase the risk of diverticulitis or its complications. In fact, these foods are excellent sources of dietary fiber, which REDUCES diverticulitis risk. Restricting them provides no benefit and may reduce your fiber intake.

faqs.question: Do I need antibiotics for diverticulitis?
faqs.answer: For uncomplicated diverticulitis (no abscess, no perforation, no peritonitis), multiple RCTs have shown that antibiotics do NOT improve outcomes compared to supportive care without antibiotics. Current guidelines recommend treating uncomplicated diverticulitis with a liquid diet and pain management (acetaminophen) without routine antibiotics. However, antibiotics ARE indicated for complicated diverticulitis (abscess >4 cm) and for immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: How is diverticulitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on severity: Uncomplicated diverticulitis — supportive care (liquid diet, acetaminophen, monitoring) with or without antibiotics. Diverticular abscess >4 cm — antibiotics plus CT-guided percutaneous drainage. Free perforation with peritonitis — urgent surgery (sigmoid colectomy). Recurrent diverticulitis — elective sigmoid colectomy after 2+ episodes or after complications requiring drainage.

faqs.question: Can I prevent another episode of diverticulitis?
faqs.answer: Yes — a high-fiber diet (25-35 g/day), regular aerobic exercise, maintaining healthy weight, avoiding smoking, and avoiding routine NSAID use are all evidence-based strategies for reducing diverticulitis recurrence. These lifestyle modifications address the underlying mechanisms (intracolonic pressure, visceral obesity, chronic inflammation) that drive recurrent episodes.

faqs.question: Will I need surgery for diverticulitis?
faqs.answer: Most patients (80-90%) with acute diverticulitis never need surgery. You may need surgery if you have 2+ recurrent episodes, a diverticular abscess that fails percutaneous drainage, free perforation with peritonitis, a bowel obstruction from stricture, or a fistula (such as colovesical fistula). Emergency surgery is required only for perforated diverticulitis with peritonitis.

faqs.question: When can I have a colonoscopy after diverticulitis?
faqs.answer: Colonoscopy should be performed 6-8 weeks after an episode of acute diverticulitis resolves. This is to exclude colorectal cancer or advanced adenomas, which can mimic diverticulitis. Colonoscopy is CONTRAINDICATED during acute diverticulitis due to the risk of perforation from the inflamed bowel wall.

faqs.question: What is the difference between diverticulosis and diverticulitis?
faqs.answer: Diverticulosis is the presence of diverticula (small outpouchings of the colon wall) WITHOUT inflammation — most people with diverticulosis have no idea they have it. Diverticulitis is inflammation of those diverticula, with symptoms of pain, fever, and infection. Diverticulosis can progress to diverticulitis when a diverticulum becomes obstructed and infected.

faqs.question: Does stress cause diverticulitis?
faqs.answer: Stress does not directly cause diverticulitis, but it can affect gut motility, bowel habits, and pain perception. People with chronic GI symptoms from diverticular disease may find that stress worsens their symptoms through gut-brain axis effects. Managing stress through mindfulness, exercise, and relaxation techniques can help with chronic diverticular symptoms.

faqs.question: Can diverticulitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes — the recurrence rate after a first episode of diverticulitis is 15-30% within 5 years. Each recurrence increases the risk of the next. The risk is highest in the first year after the initial episode. After a second episode, many surgeons recommend elective colectomy to prevent further recurrences and complications.

faqs.question: Is diverticulitis linked to cancer?
faqs.answer: Diverticulitis and colorectal cancer are separate diseases, but they can present identically and can coexist. This is why colonoscopy after recovery is essential. Having diverticulosis does NOT increase colorectal cancer risk per se, but the symptoms of cancer can be misattributed to diverticulitis — which is why colonoscopy follow-up is mandatory.

faqs.question: What foods should I eat to prevent diverticulitis?
faqs.answer: A high-fiber diet (25-35 g/day) is the most evidence-based dietary intervention. Good sources: whole grains (oats, barley, whole wheat bread, quinoa), legumes (lentils, chickpeas, black beans), fruits with skin (apples, pears), vegetables (broccoli, spinach, carrots), and nuts and seeds (which are NOT harmful despite the myth). Fiber softens stool, reduces colonic pressure, and reduces recurrence risk.

metaTitle: Diverticulitis: Symptoms, Causes, Treatment & Prevention | aihealz
metaDescription: Diverticulitis: acute colonic inflammation from infected diverticula. Learn about symptoms, Hinchey classification, treatment with/without antibiotics, surgery, and prevention. Reviewed by our medical team.

images.altText: Colon with multiple diverticula showing outpouchings through the muscular colon wall
images.caption: Diverticulitis: diverticula are small pouches that herniate through the colon wall, most commonly in the sigmoid colon. When obstructed and infected, they become inflamed — diverticulitis.

images.altText: Sigmoid colon showing diverticular outpouchings with inflammation indicating diverticulitis
images.caption: The sigmoid colon is the most common site of diverticula and diverticulitis in Western populations, presenting with classic left lower quadrant pain.

images.altText: CT scan of the abdomen showing diverticulitis with bowel wall thickening and pericolic inflammation
images.caption: CT abdomen with contrast is the diagnostic gold standard for acute diverticulitis, showing colonic wall thickening, pericolic fat stranding, and classifying disease severity.
